archetypes.name: Drought Risk
archetypes.logic: Water stress and droughts affect resource sectors.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Drought Risk” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget:
Severe droughts are expected to occur more frequently, which may amplify existing environmental issues such as land degradation and desertification and associated issues such as dust storms (World Bank 2021). At the same time, the intensity of extreme rainfall events is expected to increase with rising temperatures (Westra et al. 2014), resulting in more intense and frequent flooding and mudflows. The number of weather-related emergencies increased from 39 in 2012 to 130 in 2021 (ADB & World Bank, 2021) and forecasts indicate that the frequency of mudflows could increase by a factor of 10. 

A 5–15 percent increase in the aridity of the growing season and associated
increase in drought occurrence are the most significant adverse impacts on agriculture arising from climate
change.
archetypes.exampleGuidance:
Drought Risk: Severe droughts are expected to occur more frequently, which may amplify existing environmental issues such as land degradation and desertification and associated issues such as dust storms (World Bank 2021). At the same time, the intensity of extreme rainfall events is expected to increase with rising temperatures (Westra et al. 2014), resulting in more intense and frequent flooding and mudflows. The number of weather-related emergencies increased from 39 in 2012 to 130 in 2021 (ADB & World Bank, 2021) and forecasts indicate that the frequency of mudflows could increase by a factor of 10. 

A 5–15 percent increase in the aridity of the growing season and associated
increase in drought occurrence are the most significant adverse impacts on agriculture arising from climate
change.

archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “ESG Ratings Influence” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: ESG ratings from major providers such as MSCI and Sustainalytics are used by institutional investors to assess sustainability-related risks and opportunities across thousands of listed companies.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: ESG Ratings Influence: ESG ratings from major providers such as MSCI and Sustainalytics are used by institutional investors to assess sustainability-related risks and opportunities across thousands of listed companies.

archetypes.name: Sustainable Finance Growth
archetypes.logic: Access to capital increasingly linked to ESG performance.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Sustainable Finance Growth” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget:
Global sustainable debt issuance exceeded USD 1 trillion annually, while sustainable investment assets remain in the tens of trillions of dollars globally.

Total signatory assets under management (AUM) stand at USD 139.6 trillion, spread across more than 5,200 financial institutions globally.
archetypes.exampleGuidance:
Sustainable Finance Growth: Global sustainable debt issuance exceeded USD 1 trillion annually, while sustainable investment assets remain in the tens of trillions of dollars globally.

Total signatory assets under management (AUM) stand at USD 139.6 trillion, spread across more than 5,200 financial institutions globally.

archetypes.name: Green Bonds
archetypes.logic: Green financing requires ESG disclosures and reporting.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Green Bonds” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Annual global green bond issuance reached around USD 600 billion in 2024.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Green Bonds: Annual global green bond issuance reached around USD 600 billion in 2024.

archetypes.name: Sustainability-Linked Finance
archetypes.logic: Financing costs increasingly linked to ESG KPIs.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Sustainability-Linked Finance” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Sustainability-linked bonds and loans have become an established segment of sustainable finance, linking financing terms to predefined ESG and climate performance targets.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Sustainability-Linked Finance: Sustainability-linked bonds and loans have become an established segment of sustainable finance, linking financing terms to predefined ESG and climate performance targets.

archetypes.name: GFANZ Expectations
archetypes.logic: Investors expect portfolio decarbonization.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “GFANZ Expectations” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Unites over 450 financial institutions across 45 countries, representing more than USD $130 trillion in assets. These members commit to aligning lending and investment portfolios with net-zero emissions by 2050, using science-based interim targets for 2030 to drive real-economy decarbonization.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: GFANZ Expectations: Unites over 450 financial institutions across 45 countries, representing more than USD $130 trillion in assets. These members commit to aligning lending and investment portfolios with net-zero emissions by 2050, using science-based interim targets for 2030 to drive real-economy decarbonization.

archetypes.name: Real Estate Transition Alignment
archetypes.logic: Investors increasingly require 1.5°C-aligned pathways.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Real Estate Transition Alignment” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: CRREM provides science-based decarbonization pathways aligned with a 1.5°C scenario for real estate assets and portfolios.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Real Estate Transition Alignment: CRREM provides science-based decarbonization pathways aligned with a 1.5°C scenario for real estate assets and portfolios.

archetypes.name: Banking Climate Risk Management
archetypes.logic: Climate risk increasingly treated as financial risk.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Banking Climate Risk Management” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget:
More than 140 central banks and supervisors participate in the NGFS, promoting climate-related financial risk management and supervision.

To protect the financial system from climate-related destabilization, the FSB developed a comprehensive Roadmap targeting four key areas: firm-level disclosures, data, vulnerabilities analysis, and regulatory practices.
archetypes.exampleGuidance:
Banking Climate Risk Management: More than 140 central banks and supervisors participate in the NGFS, promoting climate-related financial risk management and supervision.

To protect the financial system from climate-related destabilization, the FSB developed a comprehensive Roadmap targeting four key areas: firm-level disclosures, data, vulnerabilities analysis, and regulatory practices.

archetypes.name: Net Zero 2050 financing demand
archetypes.logic: Banks increasingly finance renewable energy, green infrastructure and transition projects.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Net Zero 2050 financing demand” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: UAE Net Zero by 2050 Strategic Initiative; Energy Strategy 2050.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Net Zero 2050 financing demand: UAE Net Zero by 2050 Strategic Initiative; Energy Strategy 2050.

archetypes.name: Green and sustainable debt market growth
archetypes.logic: Rising issuance of green bonds and sukuk creates demand for ESG advisory and verification services.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Green and sustainable debt market growth” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget:
Expansion of sustainable finance initiatives through UAE exchanges and regulators.

The Dubai Financial Market updated its comprehensive ESG Reporting Guide, laying out 32 standardized metrics for listed companies in alignment with global frameworks like the GRI, TCFD, and ISSB protocols

The Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange continues to require and evaluate ESG disclosures using its proprietary guidance, aligning 31 key performance indicators with the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the World Federation of Exchanges (WFE).

Abu Dhabi Global Market spearheads strict sustainable finance frameworks requiring eligible companies to adhere to defined climate transition criteria and mandatory environmental sustainability attestation.

Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA): The SCA actively enforces and refines ESG disclosure mandates for listed companies, working in tandem with the UAE Sustainable Finance Working Group to mitigate climate-related financial risks.
archetypes.exampleGuidance:
Green and sustainable debt market growth: Expansion of sustainable finance initiatives through UAE exchanges and regulators.

The Dubai Financial Market updated its comprehensive ESG Reporting Guide, laying out 32 standardized metrics for listed companies in alignment with global frameworks like the GRI, TCFD, and ISSB protocols

The Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange continues to require and evaluate ESG disclosures using its proprietary guidance, aligning 31 key performance indicators with the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the World Federation of Exchanges (WFE).

Abu Dhabi Global Market spearheads strict sustainable finance frameworks requiring eligible companies to adhere to defined climate transition criteria and mandatory environmental sustainability attestation.

Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA): The SCA actively enforces and refines ESG disclosure mandates for listed companies, working in tandem with the UAE Sustainable Finance Working Group to mitigate climate-related financial risks.

archetypes.name: Green Financing Framework (2024)
archetypes.logic:
Establishes framework for sovereign green financing and increases demand for ESG-compliant projects and reporting.
International lenders increasingly require climate disclosures, governance practices and transition plans before financing projects.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Green Financing Framework (2024)” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Green Financing Framework aligned with ICMA Green Bond Principles.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Green Financing Framework (2024): Green Financing Framework aligned with ICMA Green Bond Principles.

archetypes.name: CMA Sustainable Debt Guidelines
archetypes.logic: Enables issuance of green, social, sustainability and sustainability-linked bonds and sukuk, increasing demand for ESG reporting and verification.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “CMA Sustainable Debt Guidelines” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Among the top 100 companies listed on the Saudi Main Market, the sustainability disclosure rate increased to 65% in 2024, compared to 58% in 2023 - demonstrating a growing commitment to transparency and sustainability principles
archetypes.exampleGuidance: CMA Sustainable Debt Guidelines: Among the top 100 companies listed on the Saudi Main Market, the sustainability disclosure rate increased to 65% in 2024, compared to 58% in 2023 - demonstrating a growing commitment to transparency and sustainability principles

archetypes.name: Foreign direct investment (FDI) competitiveness
archetypes.logic: ESG performance increasingly influences foreign investment decisions and supports Saudi Arabia's ambition to become a global investment hub.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Foreign direct investment (FDI) competitiveness” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Vision 2030 aims to attract international investment and improve business competitiveness. Vision 2030 set a goal of an increase in FDI to $100 billion, or 5.9% of gross domestic product, by 2030.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Foreign direct investment (FDI) competitiveness: Vision 2030 aims to attract international investment and improve business competitiveness. Vision 2030 set a goal of an increase in FDI to $100 billion, or 5.9% of gross domestic product, by 2030.

archetypes.name: Green Bonds and Sustainable Finance Development
archetypes.logic: Growth of green financing instruments increases demand for ESG reporting, project evaluation and verification.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Green Bonds and Sustainable Finance Development” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget:
International Finance Corporation (IFC): Focuses heavily on structuring private sector PPPs for large-scale renewable energy (like major solar and battery storage facilities).

Asian Development Bank (ADB): Provides critical policy-based loans to strengthen the institutional frameworks needed for climate transitions. 

European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD): Operates the Green Economy Financing Facility (GEFF), which supplies specific credit lines to local banks (e.g., Ipoteka Bank, Hamkorbank) for on-lending to SMEs.
archetypes.exampleGuidance:
Green Bonds and Sustainable Finance Development: International Finance Corporation (IFC): Focuses heavily on structuring private sector PPPs for large-scale renewable energy (like major solar and battery storage facilities).

Asian Development Bank (ADB): Provides critical policy-based loans to strengthen the institutional frameworks needed for climate transitions. 

European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD): Operates the Green Economy Financing Facility (GEFF), which supplies specific credit lines to local banks (e.g., Ipoteka Bank, Hamkorbank) for on-lending to SMEs.

archetypes.name: Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) competitiveness
archetypes.logic: Strong ESG performance improves access to international investment as Uzbekistan continues market liberalization and privatization reforms.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) competitiveness” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Government aims to attract significant foreign investment ($250 bn) under Uzbekistan-2030.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) competitiveness: Government aims to attract significant foreign investment ($250 bn) under Uzbekistan-2030.

archetypes.name: MDB Financing Influence
archetypes.logic: Development banks require ESG standards.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “MDB Financing Influence” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: The World Bank, EBRD, ADB, and IFC collectively provide billions of dollars annually in financing for infrastructure, energy, and private-sector development projects, typically requiring compliance with environmental and social standards.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: MDB Financing Influence: The World Bank, EBRD, ADB, and IFC collectively provide billions of dollars annually in financing for infrastructure, energy, and private-sector development projects, typically requiring compliance with environmental and social standards.

archetypes.name: Climate-Related Supply Chain Losses
archetypes.logic: Climate disruptions increasingly affect suppliers.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Climate-Related Supply Chain Losses” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: 215 biggest global companies report almost US$1 trillion at risk from climate impacts, with many likely to hit within the next 5 years
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Climate-Related Supply Chain Losses: 215 biggest global companies report almost US$1 trillion at risk from climate impacts, with many likely to hit within the next 5 years

archetypes.name: Scope 3 Emissions Pressure
archetypes.logic: Value-chain emissions increasingly scrutinized.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Scope 3 Emissions Pressure” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Value chain emissions account for more than 90% of emissions from the Capital Goods sector
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Scope 3 Emissions Pressure: Value chain emissions account for more than 90% of emissions from the Capital Goods sector

archetypes.name: Human Rights Due Diligence
archetypes.logic: Customers increasingly require supplier ESG compliance.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Human Rights Due Diligence” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: The OECD Due Diligence Guidance and the EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) establish expectations for identifying and addressing human rights and environmental impacts across value chains.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Human Rights Due Diligence: The OECD Due Diligence Guidance and the EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) establish expectations for identifying and addressing human rights and environmental impacts across value chains.

archetypes.name: Carbon pricing and CBAM exposure
archetypes.logic: Exporters supplying European markets increasingly need product-level emissions accounting and decarbonization strategies to remain competitive.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Carbon pricing and CBAM exposure” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: EU CBAM affects aluminium, iron, steel, cement and related value chains.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Carbon pricing and CBAM exposure: EU CBAM affects aluminium, iron, steel, cement and related value chains.

archetypes.name: Responsible mineral supply chains
archetypes.logic: Global buyers increasingly require traceability, responsible sourcing and ESG assurance throughout mineral supply chains.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Responsible mineral supply chains” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Vision 2030 mining strategy emphasizes integrated mineral value chains and international competitiveness.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Responsible mineral supply chains: Vision 2030 mining strategy emphasizes integrated mineral value chains and international competitiveness.

archetypes.name: Localization (In-Kingdom Value Add / IKTVA)
archetypes.logic: Expanding local supplier requirements encourage stronger ESG management throughout domestic supply chains and contractor networks.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Localization (In-Kingdom Value Add / IKTVA)” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Vision 2030 industrial localization objectives.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Localization (In-Kingdom Value Add / IKTVA): Vision 2030 industrial localization objectives.
keyPublishers: National Industrial Development and Logistics Program

archetypes.name: EcoVadis Supplier Assessment
archetypes.logic: Buyers increasingly screen suppliers using ESG ratings.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “EcoVadis Supplier Assessment” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: More than 150,000 companies have been rated through the EcoVadis platform
archetypes.exampleGuidance: EcoVadis Supplier Assessment: More than 150,000 companies have been rated through the EcoVadis platform

archetypes.name: Responsible Minerals Sourcing
archetypes.logic: OEMs require traceable, responsibly produced minerals.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Responsible Minerals Sourcing” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: The Responsible Minerals Initiative has over 500 member companies supporting responsible mineral supply chains.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Responsible Minerals Sourcing: The Responsible Minerals Initiative has over 500 member companies supporting responsible mineral supply chains.

archetypes.name: Sustainable Aviation Fuel Adoption
archetypes.logic: Key decarbonization solution for aviation.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Sustainable Aviation Fuel Adoption” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) is expected to contribute approximately 65% of the emissions reductions needed for aviation to reach net zero by 2050.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Sustainable Aviation Fuel Adoption: Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) is expected to contribute approximately 65% of the emissions reductions needed for aviation to reach net zero by 2050.

archetypes.name: Shipping Fuel Transition
archetypes.logic: Industry moving toward low-carbon fuels.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Shipping Fuel Transition” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: The IMO adopted a strategy targeting net-zero GHG emissions from international shipping by or around 2050, supported by progressively stricter fuel standards.
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Shipping Fuel Transition: The IMO adopted a strategy targeting net-zero GHG emissions from international shipping by or around 2050, supported by progressively stricter fuel standards.

archetypes.name: SBTi Buildings Criteria – Mandatory Adoption Threshold
archetypes.logic: Companies that own, develop, manage or finance real estate and whose in-use operational building emissions represent ≥20% of total Scope 1, 2 & 3 (cat. 1–14) emissions in their chosen base year must comply fully with the SBTi Buildings Criteria and set a validated target. Non-compliance blocks SBTi approval.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “SBTi Buildings Criteria – Mandatory Adoption Threshold” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % of Scope 1+2+3 emissions from buildings; SBTi target validation status
archetypes.exampleGuidance: SBTi Buildings Criteria – Mandatory Adoption Threshold: % of Scope 1+2+3 emissions from buildings; SBTi target validation status

archetypes.name: Upfront Embodied Emissions Target Obligation
archetypes.logic: Companies whose upfront embodied emissions from new developments or first-owner acquisitions exceeded 20% of total Scope 1, 2 & 3 in any one year within the prior 3 years must set a dedicated upfront embodied emissions target. Base year must be no earlier than 3 years before target submission.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Upfront Embodied Emissions Target Obligation” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: kgCO₂e/m² upfront embodied carbon intensity; % reduction vs base year; base year no older than 3 years
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Upfront Embodied Emissions Target Obligation: kgCO₂e/m² upfront embodied carbon intensity; % reduction vs base year; base year no older than 3 years

archetypes.name: Whole Building Approach – Mandatory Scope 3 Coverage
archetypes.logic: All in-use operational emissions from both landlord- and tenant-controlled spaces must be included in target boundaries, regardless of GHG boundary consolidation method. Owner-lessors must include Scope 3 Category 13 (downstream leased assets). Property managers must apply whole-building approach when setting SDA targets.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Whole Building Approach – Mandatory Scope 3 Coverage” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % of portfolio floor area with whole-building energy data; Scope 3 Cat.13 reported (tCO₂e)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Whole Building Approach – Mandatory Scope 3 Coverage: % of portfolio floor area with whole-building energy data; Scope 3 Cat.13 reported (tCO₂e)

archetypes.name: Fugitive Emissions Inclusion
archetypes.logic: Fugitive emissions (e.g., refrigerant leakage from HVAC/cooling systems) from all building types must be included within in-use operational emissions and reported in both GHG inventory and target boundaries.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Fugitive Emissions Inclusion” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Fugitive emissions (tCO₂e) by building type; refrigerant tracking systems in place (% portfolio)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Fugitive Emissions Inclusion: Fugitive emissions (tCO₂e) by building type; refrigerant tracking systems in place (% portfolio)

archetypes.name: CRREM Pathway Alignment & Stranding Risk
archetypes.logic: SBTi in-use operational pathways are derived from CRREM (Carbon Risk Real Estate Monitor). Users must select the most appropriate building typology and geography from the SBTi Buildings Target-Setting Tool. Assets not aligned with a 1.5°C CRREM pathway face obsolescence and stranding risk as regulations tighten.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “CRREM Pathway Alignment & Stranding Risk” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % of portfolio floor area on a CRREM-aligned 1.5°C pathway; stranded asset exposure by geography/typology
archetypes.exampleGuidance: CRREM Pathway Alignment & Stranding Risk: % of portfolio floor area on a CRREM-aligned 1.5°C pathway; stranded asset exposure by geography/typology

archetypes.name: Upfront Embodied Carbon Disclosure – New Developments
archetypes.logic: Developers using annual construction-completion accounting (Method 1) should disclose the upfront embodied carbon intensity of completed developments as an additional transparency measure, supporting sector-wide data availability and investor benchmarking.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Upfront Embodied Carbon Disclosure – New Developments” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Upfront embodied carbon intensity (kgCO₂e/m²) of completed developments by year
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Upfront Embodied Carbon Disclosure – New Developments: Upfront embodied carbon intensity (kgCO₂e/m²) of completed developments by year

archetypes.name: No New Fossil Fuel Equipment by 2030
archetypes.logic: Companies required to use SBTi Buildings Criteria must publicly commit to install no new fossil fuel equipment (space heating, cooking, hot water, power generation) owned or financially controlled in their buildings portfolios from 2030 at the latest. At end-of-life, fossil systems must be replaced with non-fossil alternatives. Emergency/back-up systems and regulatory-critical uses are exempt.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “No New Fossil Fuel Equipment by 2030” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Date of public no-new-fossil-fuel commitment; % of portfolio with fossil fuel systems; replacement roadmap
archetypes.exampleGuidance: No New Fossil Fuel Equipment by 2030: Date of public no-new-fossil-fuel commitment; % of portfolio with fossil fuel systems; replacement roadmap

archetypes.name: In-Use Operational SDA – Mandatory Method for Owners
archetypes.logic: Owner-occupiers and owner-lessors must use the buildings in-use operational Sectoral Decarbonization Approach (SDA), irrespective of whether emissions are categorized as Scope 1, 2 or 3. Property managers may use SDA or other permitted methods. Intensity pathway must be expressed in kgCO₂e/m².
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “In-Use Operational SDA – Mandatory Method for Owners” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: kgCO₂e/m² operational emissions intensity; % reduction trajectory vs SDA pathway; floor area (m²) under management
archetypes.exampleGuidance: In-Use Operational SDA – Mandatory Method for Owners: kgCO₂e/m² operational emissions intensity; % reduction trajectory vs SDA pathway; floor area (m²) under management

archetypes.name: Location-Based Emissions Mandatory Disclosure
archetypes.logic: If the market-based accounting approach is chosen for target-setting, companies must also measure and report whole-building in-use operational emissions using the location-based approach as a separate mandatory line item in their annual GHG inventory. This prevents greenwashing through renewable energy certificate (REC) procurement.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Location-Based Emissions Mandatory Disclosure” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Location-based vs market-based Scope 2 delta (tCO₂e); location-based operational intensity (kgCO₂e/m²)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Location-Based Emissions Mandatory Disclosure: Location-based vs market-based Scope 2 delta (tCO₂e); location-based operational intensity (kgCO₂e/m²)

archetypes.name: Portfolio-Level Target Aggregation Requirement
archetypes.logic: Companies with portfolios spanning multiple geographies and/or building typologies must aggregate sub-targets into a single overall reduction target using the SBTi Buildings Target-Setting Tool. In-use operational and upfront embodied targets must be kept separate. Sub-target evidence must be submitted to SBTi validation.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Portfolio-Level Target Aggregation Requirement” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Number of sub-targets by geography/typology; portfolio-level intensity reduction (%); SBTi tool outputs submitted
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Portfolio-Level Target Aggregation Requirement: Number of sub-targets by geography/typology; portfolio-level intensity reduction (%); SBTi tool outputs submitted

archetypes.name: User Category Self-Classification & Justification
archetypes.logic: Companies must identify and apply criteria for all applicable user categories (Developer, Owner-Occupier, Owner-Lessor, Property Manager, Financial Institution) that describe their business activities, and briefly justify their categorization when submitting for SBTi validation. Misclassification can result in non-conformance.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “User Category Self-Classification & Justification” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: User category declared; scope 3 categories reported per user type; validation conformance status
archetypes.exampleGuidance: User Category Self-Classification & Justification: User category declared; scope 3 categories reported per user type; validation conformance status

archetypes.name: Asset Stranding Risk from Carbon Pathway Misalignment
archetypes.logic: Buildings that are not on a 1.5°C-aligned CRREM decarbonization pathway face increasing risk of becoming stranded assets as carbon pricing, energy codes, and green building regulations tighten. Retrofitting costs and potential loss of asset value are material financial risks for real estate owners and lenders.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Asset Stranding Risk from Carbon Pathway Misalignment” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % portfolio aligned to 1.5°C CRREM pathway; estimated retrofit CAPEX ($/m²); carbon stranding year by asset
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Asset Stranding Risk from Carbon Pathway Misalignment: % portfolio aligned to 1.5°C CRREM pathway; estimated retrofit CAPEX ($/m²); carbon stranding year by asset

archetypes.name: Fossil Fuel Lock-In Risk
archetypes.logic: Installation of new fossil fuel heating, cooling or power systems creates long-lived capital lock-in (typically 15–25 year asset lifetimes), exposing companies to stranded asset risk as the 2030 no-new-fossil commitment deadline approaches and jurisdictions phase out gas boilers and fossil HVAC.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Fossil Fuel Lock-In Risk” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % of portfolio with fossil fuel systems installed post-2024; remaining useful life of fossil equipment; planned replacement schedule
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Fossil Fuel Lock-In Risk: % of portfolio with fossil fuel systems installed post-2024; remaining useful life of fossil equipment; planned replacement schedule

archetypes.name: Embodied Carbon Exposure in New Construction
archetypes.logic: Upfront embodied emissions from new construction (materials, manufacturing, transport) are locked in at point of build and cannot be reduced post-completion. Companies with high development pipelines face growing regulatory and financial exposure as embodied carbon limits are introduced in building codes globally.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Embodied Carbon Exposure in New Construction” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Upfront embodied carbon intensity (kgCO₂e/m²) of pipeline; % of developments with whole-life carbon assessments
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Embodied Carbon Exposure in New Construction: Upfront embodied carbon intensity (kgCO₂e/m²) of pipeline; % of developments with whole-life carbon assessments

archetypes.name: Grid Decarbonisation Dependency Risk
archetypes.logic: In-use operational emissions intensity is partly a function of grid carbon intensity. Companies using market-based accounting (RECs/PPAs) may overstate decarbonisation progress if grid decarbonisation projections are not incorporated. SBTi recommends location-based accounting and use of future grid projections.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Grid Decarbonisation Dependency Risk” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Location-based vs market-based Scope 2 gap (tCO₂e); grid carbon intensity (gCO₂/kWh) by market; REC/PPA coverage (%)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Grid Decarbonisation Dependency Risk: Location-based vs market-based Scope 2 gap (tCO₂e); grid carbon intensity (gCO₂/kWh) by market; REC/PPA coverage (%)

archetypes.name: Financial Institution Financed Emissions – Buildings SDA Obligation
archetypes.logic: FIs with material buildings-related financed emissions (Scope 3 Cat. 15) that use the SDA methodology must apply the whole-building approach, accounting for their proportional ownership share of operational emissions. Equity REITs must follow the corporate target validation route. Coverage must reach ≥67% of base-year financed emissions.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Financial Institution Financed Emissions – Buildings SDA Obligation” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Financed buildings emissions (tCO₂e, Scope 3 Cat.15); % financed emissions covered by SDA target; WACI (tCO₂e/m²)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Financial Institution Financed Emissions – Buildings SDA Obligation: Financed buildings emissions (tCO₂e, Scope 3 Cat.15); % financed emissions covered by SDA target; WACI (tCO₂e/m²)

archetypes.name: Financed Upfront Embodied Carbon Disclosure (FIs)
archetypes.logic: Financial institutions should disclose the financed upfront embodied carbon intensity of buildings they acquire as owners or re/finance. This supports transition risk assessment of lending and investment portfolios and responds to growing investor and regulatory demand for portfolio-level embodied carbon transparency.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Financed Upfront Embodied Carbon Disclosure (FIs)” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Financed upfront embodied carbon intensity (kgCO₂e/m²); % of real estate loan/investment book with embodied carbon data
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Financed Upfront Embodied Carbon Disclosure (FIs): Financed upfront embodied carbon intensity (kgCO₂e/m²); % of real estate loan/investment book with embodied carbon data

archetypes.name: Absolute Emissions Disclosure for Intensity-Target Users
archetypes.logic: Companies setting intensity-based targets should also disclose the absolute emissions reductions expected from those targets. This ensures investors can assess whether intensity improvements translate into real-world emissions reductions or are offset by portfolio growth.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Absolute Emissions Disclosure for Intensity-Target Users” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Absolute Scope 1+2+3 emissions trajectory (tCO₂e); absolute reduction implied by intensity target (%)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Absolute Emissions Disclosure for Intensity-Target Users: Absolute Scope 1+2+3 emissions trajectory (tCO₂e); absolute reduction implied by intensity target (%)

archetypes.name: Like-for-Like vs Divestment Emissions Decomposition
archetypes.logic: Companies should separately disclose: (1) emissions reductions from genuine decarbonisation of retained assets (like-for-like), and (2) absolute emissions from assets divested during the reporting period. This allows investors to distinguish authentic operational improvement from portfolio reshuffling.

archetypes.name: Fossil Fuel Expansion Policy Restrictions
archetypes.logic: SBTi Financial Institutions Net-Zero Standard (V1.0, July 2025) defines O&G companies 'in transition' as those not engaging in new fossil fuel expansion. This creates a regulatory-level exclusion criterion that will gate access to institutional lending and investment for expansion-focused operators, including NOCs in KZ, SA, UAE and UZ.

archetypes.name: Paris Agreement Carbon Budget Constraint
archetypes.logic: By end-2024, ~160 Gt CO₂ of the post-2020 500 Gt CO₂ budget had been emitted, leaving ~340 Gt CO₂ — exhaustible in under a decade at current rates. This hard physical constraint underpins all regulatory and standard-setting activity and sets the macro regulatory trajectory for the sector.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Paris Agreement Carbon Budget Constraint” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Remaining carbon budget: ~340 Gt CO₂ (post-2024); current annual global emissions vs. remaining budget
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Paris Agreement Carbon Budget Constraint: Remaining carbon budget: ~340 Gt CO₂ (post-2024); current annual global emissions vs. remaining budget

archetypes.name: Stranded Asset Risk from New Upstream Projects
countryScopes: UZ and other major producing nations
archetypes.logic: Existing global oil and gas reserves, if fully exploited, already exceed the 1.5°C carbon budget. New upstream projects therefore face high stranded-asset risk as policy tightens. ESG due diligence must flag capex committed to new long-lead-time projects as a material transition risk.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Stranded Asset Risk from New Upstream Projects” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % capex allocated to new upstream vs. existing fields; reserve life index; stranded asset exposure ($ bn)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Stranded Asset Risk from New Upstream Projects: % capex allocated to new upstream vs. existing fields; reserve life index; stranded asset exposure ($ bn)

archetypes.name: Lock-in Risk from Long-Lived Infrastructure
countryScopes: UZ — all with long-cycle upstream and midstream assets)
archetypes.logic: Oil and gas assets have long lifetimes and high capital intensity, creating infrastructure lock-in that constrains transition speed. Companies approving new long-lived assets today risk misalignment with 2030/2035 policy milestones — a key qualitative ESG risk factor.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Lock-in Risk from Long-Lived Infrastructure” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Average remaining asset life (years); % of asset base commissioned post-2023; capex in assets with >20-year life
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Lock-in Risk from Long-Lived Infrastructure: Average remaining asset life (years); % of asset base commissioned post-2023; capex in assets with >20-year life

archetypes.name: Business Model Misalignment — Continued Fossil Production
archetypes.logic: The report explicitly classifies 'continued production of fossil fuels for energy use' (Model #3) as 'Not Aligned' with net-zero transition. Companies pursuing this model without credible diversification plans carry the highest ESG transition risk and should be flagged in portfolio screening.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Business Model Misalignment — Continued Fossil Production” for the selected country and sector?

archetypes.name: Regional Pace of O&G Decline — Differential Exposure
archetypes.logic: IEA NZE 2023 regional data shows gas production falling 92% in Europe but only 59% in the Middle East by 2050, and oil 89% in Europe vs 62% in Middle East. KZ and UZ sit in regions with moderate declines (~75–84%). This differential creates region-specific physical transition risk for asset owners.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Regional Pace of O&G Decline — Differential Exposure” for the selected country and sector?

archetypes.exampleGuidance: Regional Pace of O&G Decline — Differential Exposure: Regional production decline % by 2050 (IEA NZE): Europe gas -92%, Middle East gas -59%, Eurasia gas -84%; oil similar pattern

archetypes.name: CCS Dependency Risk
countryScopes: Global (especially producers relying on CCS: Middle East
archetypes.logic: Scenarios relying heavily on Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) to justify continued fossil production (e.g., Shell Sky 2050, CWF Central) assume large-scale CDR deployment that remains techno-economically uncertain. Excessive CCS reliance is flagged as a credibility risk in SBTi's assessment — a material ESG risk for companies using CCS to defer transition.

archetypes.name: Overshoot Pathway Risk — High vs. Low Overshoot Scenarios
archetypes.logic: Some corporate scenarios (Shell Sky 2050, BP Net Zero 2020, Equinor) claim 1.5°C alignment but independent analysis (Brecha et al. 2022) shows they exceed the carbon budget and are Paris-inconsistent. Using such scenarios as benchmarks for ESG target-setting carries regulatory and reputational risk.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Overshoot Pathway Risk — High vs. Low Overshoot Scenarios” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Independent temperature assessment of scenario (°C end-century); carbon budget consumed vs. IPCC C1 category
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Overshoot Pathway Risk — High vs. Low Overshoot Scenarios: Independent temperature assessment of scenario (°C end-century); carbon budget consumed vs. IPCC C1 category

archetypes.logic: Global energy investment in 2025 is projected at ~USD 3.3 trillion, of which USD 2.2 trillion (67%) targets low-carbon energy — roughly double unabated fossil fuel spend. Electricity-sector investment already exceeds oil, gas and coal combined. This capital reallocation is the primary financial market ESG driver for the sector.

countryScopes: UZ and all major upstream-focused producers
archetypes.logic: Upstream oil and gas investment (~USD 570 bn in 2024) is expected to stabilise or decline slightly under IEA projections. For ESG-focused investors, any material increase in upstream capex by portfolio companies signals misalignment with 1.5°C pathways and should trigger engagement or divestment.

archetypes.logic: The SBTi report proposes 'low-carbon energy revenue ratio' (revenues from low-carbon energy / total revenue) as a non-emission ESG metric. This KPI provides a leading indicator of transition readiness and is particularly relevant for integrated oil companies and NOCs diversifying into renewables.

archetypes.logic: SBTi's Corporate Net-Zero Standard V2 research explores 'alignment' and 'non-emission' targets (e.g., production volume targets, investment ratios) that may replace or supplement quantitative Scope 3 targets. This signals a shift in capital markets ESG assessment toward actionable, verifiable metrics beyond pure emissions accounting.

archetypes.logic: The report explicitly supports adjacent frameworks for 'assessing fossil fuel exposure and transition alignment' — e.g., NGFS scenarios used by financial regulators. archetypes.logic: Operational CO₂ (Scope 1+2) from refining, transport and power use for operations is a practical near-term decarbonisation lever via electrification and efficiency improvements. IEA NZE 2023 shows sectoral operational emissions must fall >60% by 2030 across the O&G supply chain.

archetypes.logic: O&G methane reductions account for >two-thirds of total energy-system methane reductions needed by 2030 (IEA NZE 2023). Methane mitigation across the supply chain (extraction, separation, processing, distribution) is the highest-impact operational ESG action available to producers.

archetypes.logic: The SBTi report maps distinct decarbonisation levers across upstream (production decline, CCS), midstream (electrification, H₂/CO₂ infrastructure repurposing) and downstream (low-carbon fuel substitution, demand reduction). ESG assessments must evaluate each segment separately to capture true supply-chain emission trajectories.

archetypes.name: Scrap Ratio Dependency on SDA Targets
archetypes.logic: A company's scrap ratio in both base and target years determines the steepness of its SDA emission intensity reduction pathway. A decreasing scrap ratio (e.g., expansion into ironmaking) requires regulatory justification (Steel-C9) and may signal increased transition risk exposure.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Scrap Ratio Dependency on SDA Targets” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Scrap ratio directly determines SDA intensity pathway; decreasing ratio requires written justification (Steel-C9)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Scrap Ratio Dependency on SDA Targets: Scrap ratio directly determines SDA intensity pathway; decreasing ratio requires written justification (Steel-C9)

archetypes.name: Infrastructure & Shared Energy System Risk
archetypes.logic: The majority of capital expenditure needed to decarbonise steel (green hydrogen infrastructure, CO₂ pipelines, renewable energy grids) lies outside steel plants. This creates dependency on governments and utilities, introducing infrastructure and regulatory risk beyond the steelmaker's control.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Infrastructure & Shared Energy System Risk” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Majority of decarbonisation CAPEX estimated outside steel plants (MPP, 2022)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Infrastructure & Shared Energy System Risk: Majority of decarbonisation CAPEX estimated outside steel plants (MPP, 2022)

archetypes.name: Science-Based Targets as Investor Signal
archetypes.logic: SBTs serve as the primary mechanism for companies to demonstrate climate ambition to investors and stakeholders. Companies without validated SBTs face increasing exclusion from ESG-screened portfolios and green finance instruments.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Science-Based Targets as Investor Signal” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: SBTs cited as tool to demonstrate ambition to stakeholders and call for supportive regulatory environment (p.24)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Science-Based Targets as Investor Signal: SBTs cited as tool to demonstrate ambition to stakeholders and call for supportive regulatory environment (p.24)

archetypes.name: Green Hydrogen & Low-Carbon CapEx Disclosure (Steel-R2)
archetypes.logic: SBTi recommends (Steel-R2) that steel companies disclose planned milestones and near-term investments in breakthrough technologies to demonstrate commitment credibility. This is directly linked to green bond / sustainability-linked loan frameworks requiring milestone disclosure.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Green Hydrogen & Low-Carbon CapEx Disclosure (Steel-R2)” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Recommended disclosure: R&D spend, planned investments, 'readiness for net-zero' assessments (Steel-R2)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Green Hydrogen & Low-Carbon CapEx Disclosure (Steel-R2): Recommended disclosure: R&D spend, planned investments, 'readiness for net-zero' assessments (Steel-R2)

archetypes.name: Absolute Emissions Disclosure vs Intensity Targets (Steel-R3)
archetypes.logic: Companies using intensity-based SDA targets are recommended (Steel-R3) to also publish absolute emission figures to demonstrate real reductions, not just intensity improvements driven by output growth. This is increasingly required by TCFD, ISSB IFRS S2, and investor coalitions.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Absolute Emissions Disclosure vs Intensity Targets (Steel-R3)” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Recommended: publish absolute emissions alongside intensity targets (Steel-R3)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Absolute Emissions Disclosure vs Intensity Targets (Steel-R3): Recommended: publish absolute emissions alongside intensity targets (Steel-R3)

archetypes.name: Financial Institutions — Scope 3 Cat. 15 SDA Targets
archetypes.logic: Banks and investors financing steel companies may use the iron & steel SDA to set scope 3 category 15 (investments) targets, using attributable hot rolled steel output. Default scrap share assumption of 0% applies if data unavailable. This links steel company SBTs directly to lender ESG obligations.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Financial Institutions — Scope 3 Cat. 15 SDA Targets” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: FIs use steel SDA with attribution factor; default 0% scrap if data unavailable; target wording per FI format
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Financial Institutions — Scope 3 Cat. 15 SDA Targets: FIs use steel SDA with attribution factor; default 0% scrap if data unavailable; target wording per FI format

archetypes.name: Iron Ore Supplier — Scope 3 Cat. 10 SDA Option
archetypes.logic: Iron ore suppliers may voluntarily use the iron & steel SDA to set scope 3 cat. 10 (processing of sold products) targets at 1.5 °C ambition level, covering all downstream processing to hot rolled steel. Alternatively, standard cross-sector scope 3 methods apply (7% annual physical/economic intensity reduction).
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Iron Ore Supplier — Scope 3 Cat. 10 SDA Option” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Iron ore suppliers can use SDA for cat. 10; must include full downstream boundary; alternative: 7%/yr intensity target
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Iron Ore Supplier — Scope 3 Cat. 10 SDA Option: Iron ore suppliers can use SDA for cat. 10; must include full downstream boundary; alternative: 7%/yr intensity target

archetypes.name: Ferroalloy Supplier — Separate Scope 3 Target (Steel-R1)
archetypes.logic: Ferroalloy production is excluded from the core boundary due to the lack of a recognised 1.5 °C pathway. Stainless and high-alloy steel producers should (Steel-R1) set a scope 3 cat. 1 target for ferroalloy sourcing regardless of emission share — a recommended but increasingly expected practice for high-alloy producers.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Ferroalloy Supplier — Separate Scope 3 Target (Steel-R1)” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Ferroalloy production excluded from core boundary; stainless producers recommended to set separate cat. 1 scope 3 target (Steel-R1)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Ferroalloy Supplier — Separate Scope 3 Target (Steel-R1): Ferroalloy production excluded from core boundary; stainless producers recommended to set separate cat. 1 scope 3 target (Steel-R1)

archetypes.name: Upstream Fuel & Energy Methane — Mandatory Cat. 3
archetypes.logic: All iron & steelmakers must cover 100% of scope 3 category 3 (upstream fuel- and energy-related emissions) regardless of its share of total emissions (Steel-C5). This includes methane from coal mining supply chains — a material ESG risk for steelmakers sourcing from Central Asian coal producers (Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan).
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Upstream Fuel & Energy Methane — Mandatory Cat. 3” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: 100% scope 3 cat. 3 required; covers coal mining methane, gas supply upstream (Steel-C5); data expected to improve within 2 years
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Upstream Fuel & Energy Methane — Mandatory Cat. 3: 100% scope 3 cat. 3 required; covers coal mining methane, gas supply upstream (Steel-C5); data expected to improve within 2 years

archetypes.name: Automakers & Construction — Steel Procurement SDA
archetypes.logic: Downstream buyers of steel (automakers, construction firms) may use the 'Steel SDA – for steel purchasers' tool to set scope 3 cat. 1 targets for purchased steel at 1.5 °C ambition. However, absolute targets may be more appropriate as demand reduction is a key lever for these sectors.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Automakers & Construction — Steel Procurement SDA” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Downstream buyers can set 1.5 °C-aligned scope 3 cat. 1 SDA targets for purchased steel; absolute target preferred if demand reduction is relevant
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Automakers & Construction — Steel Procurement SDA: Downstream buyers can set 1.5 °C-aligned scope 3 cat. 1 SDA targets for purchased steel; absolute target preferred if demand reduction is relevant

archetypes.name: Scope Leakage Prevention — Purchased vs Own Production
archetypes.logic: The mandatory inclusion of scope 3 emissions from purchased intermediate products (e.g., merchant HBI, coke) prevents companies from reducing apparent scope 1 by outsourcing production, while maintaining a level playing field between vertically integrated and trading-focused steelmakers.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Scope Leakage Prevention — Purchased vs Own Production” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Purchased intermediate products (HBI, coke, pig iron, sinter, pellets, oxygen, lime) must be included in core boundary target (≥95%)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Scope Leakage Prevention — Purchased vs Own Production: Purchased intermediate products (HBI, coke, pig iron, sinter, pellets, oxygen, lime) must be included in core boundary target (≥95%)

archetypes.name: Biomass Supply Chain Integrity
archetypes.logic: Use of biomass (biochar, bioenergy) as a decarbonisation lever in blast furnaces is recognised but requires compliance with SBTi Criteria C10, R3, R4 on biogenic emissions accounting. Any biomass contributing to deforestation or land-use competition must be avoided.
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Biomass Supply Chain Integrity” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Biomass GHG rules: land use + processing + combustion emissions must all be accounted per SBTi C10; deforestation-linked biomass prohibited
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Biomass Supply Chain Integrity: Biomass GHG rules: land use + processing + combustion emissions must all be accounted per SBTi C10; deforestation-linked biomass prohibited

archetypes.logic: FIs financing coal or oil & gas above defined thresholds (revenue %, exit-list membership) must adopt a structured four-pillar framework — immediate disclosure, halt of new high-risk financing, absolute upstream reduction, and full coal phaseout — closing the "soft commitment" loophole common in voluntary climate finance pledges

archetypes.evidenceTarget: Public Disclose/Halt/Transition/Phaseout policy; coal phaseout by 2030 (OECD) / 2040 (global); 4.2%+ annual upstream oil & gas emissions reduction
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Mandatory fossil fuel sector targets (Disclose/Halt/Transition/Phaseout): Public Disclose/Halt/Transition/Phaseout policy; coal phaseout by 2030 (OECD) / 2040 (global); 4.2%+ annual upstream oil & gas emissions reduction

archetypes.name: Carbon credit and avoided-emissions exclusion
archetypes.logic: Carbon credits and "avoided emissions" cannot be counted toward target progress, closing a common greenwashing pathway where FIs claim climate credit for emissions never actually reduced within their own portfolio boundary
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Carbon credit and avoided-emissions exclusion” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Confirmation in target submission that no credits/avoided emissions are counted as progress
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Carbon credit and avoided-emissions exclusion: Confirmation in target submission that no credits/avoided emissions are counted as progress

archetypes.name: Mandatory transparency on target methodology and progress
archetypes.logic: Annual disclosure of Scope 1+2 emissions, progress against all approved targets, financed-emissions methodology, and (for fossil fuel) granular financial/GHG metrics increases comparability and reduces "black box" climate claims across the sector
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Mandatory transparency on target methodology and progress” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Annual public disclosure (CDP, Net Zero Data Public Utility, sustainability report, or company website); % coverage formula reported
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Mandatory transparency on target methodology and progress: Annual public disclosure (CDP, Net Zero Data Public Utility, sustainability report, or company website); % coverage formula reported

archetypes.name: Five-year mandatory target recalculation
archetypes.logic: Targets must be reviewed/recalculated within 5 years of approval, and immediately upon material structural change (M&A, divestiture, ≥5% inventory shift), keeping ambition aligned with evolving climate science rather than allowing stale, outdated commitments to persist
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Five-year mandatory target recalculation” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Recalculation/revalidation submitted within 5 years; trigger-event disclosures
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Five-year mandatory target recalculation: Recalculation/revalidation submitted within 5 years; trigger-event disclosures

archetypes.name: Stranded-asset and transition risk in fossil fuel exposure
archetypes.logic: The Halt provision (immediate cessation of new financing to new coal mines/plants and new long-lead-time upstream oil & gas) directly signals to FIs that continued financing of fossil expansion creates acute transition and reputational risk as the global carbon budget tightens
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Stranded-asset and transition risk in fossil fuel exposure” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Exposure ($ value) to GCEL/GOGEL-listed companies and expansion-plan projects; halt-policy publication date
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Stranded-asset and transition risk in fossil fuel exposure: Exposure ($ value) to GCEL/GOGEL-listed companies and expansion-plan projects; halt-policy publication date

archetypes.name: Engagement-failure / laggard-borrower risk
archetypes.logic: The Portfolio Coverage method explicitly warns that FIs failing to phase out financing to fossil fuel clients without credible 1.5°C transition plans "expose themselves to risks of stranded assets and reputational damage," linking client engagement quality directly to the FI's own risk profile
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Engagement-failure / laggard-borrower risk” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % of fossil fuel portfolio companies with 1.5°C-aligned transition plans (recommended disclosure)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Engagement-failure / laggard-borrower risk: % of fossil fuel portfolio companies with 1.5°C-aligned transition plans (recommended disclosure)

archetypes.name: Bioenergy and land-use accounting risk
archetypes.logic: Mandatory inclusion of land-use-change emissions and removals tied to bioenergy feedstocks in the target boundary prevents FIs from using bioenergy-labeled financing as a "clean" decarbonization lever while masking embedded land-related emissions risk
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Bioenergy and land-use accounting risk” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Disclosed bioenergy footnote in target language; FLAG-related Portfolio Coverage targets for ≥20% FLAG-exposed clients
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Bioenergy and land-use accounting risk: Disclosed bioenergy footnote in target language; FLAG-related Portfolio Coverage targets for ≥20% FLAG-exposed clients

archetypes.name: Portfolio Coverage (client SBT-adoption) engagement targets
archetypes.logic: FIs can satisfy portfolio requirements by committing a rising share of borrowers/investees to set their own SBTi-validated targets (linear path to 100% by 2040), explicitly using capital allocation and engagement leverage to cascade science-based target-setting into the real economy
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Portfolio Coverage (client SBT-adoption) engagement targets” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % borrowers/investees with SBTi-approved targets vs linear 2040 pathway; weighting methodology disclosed
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Portfolio Coverage (client SBT-adoption) engagement targets: % borrowers/investees with SBTi-approved targets vs linear 2040 pathway; weighting methodology disclosed

archetypes.name: Portfolio Temperature Rating alignment
archetypes.logic: Requiring FIs to align the aggregate "temperature score" of their portfolio with Paris Agreement goals (1.5°C for Scope 1+2, well-below-2°C for Scope 1-3 by 2040) creates a continuous capital-pricing signal that rewards lower-carbon investees over time
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Portfolio Temperature Rating alignment” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Portfolio temperature score (°C) vs linear glidepath to 2040; data-provider and methodology disclosure
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Portfolio Temperature Rating alignment: Portfolio temperature score (°C) vs linear glidepath to 2040; data-provider and methodology disclosure

archetypes.name: SDA-based physical intensity targets for real assets/electricity finance
archetypes.logic: Mandatory SDA targets for real estate assets and electricity-generation project finance route capital systematically toward low-carbon-intensity assets (kgCO₂e/m², gCO₂e/kWh), since these activities have available physical-intensity benchmarks unlike most other asset classes
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “SDA-based physical intensity targets for real assets/electricity finance” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Portfolio emissions intensity vs 1.5°C sector pathway; 100 gCO₂e/kWh 2030 threshold for power
archetypes.exampleGuidance: SDA-based physical intensity targets for real assets/electricity finance: Portfolio emissions intensity vs 1.5°C sector pathway; 100 gCO₂e/kWh 2030 threshold for power

archetypes.name: Renewable electricity procurement as Scope 2 alternative
archetypes.logic: Allowing FIs to satisfy Scope 2 targets via RE100-aligned procurement (80% by 2025, 100% by 2030) ties an FI's own operational decarbonization credibility to the broader renewable-energy market, reinforcing demand-side capital flows into clean power
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Renewable electricity procurement as Scope 2 alternative” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % renewable electricity procurement vs 80%/100% thresholds
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Renewable electricity procurement as Scope 2 alternative: % renewable electricity procurement vs 80%/100% thresholds

archetypes.name: Differentiated treatment by asset-management mandate type
archetypes.logic: Distinguishing discretionary (required), advisory (optional), and execution-only (out-of-scope) mandates determines which pools of third-party capital an FI is accountable for decarbonizing, shaping how asset managers structure and market their investment products
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Differentiated treatment by asset-management mandate type” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Disclosed mandate-type classification; AIWM exclusion disclosure (banks only)
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Differentiated treatment by asset-management mandate type: Disclosed mandate-type classification; AIWM exclusion disclosure (banks only)

archetypes.name: Cascading SBT adoption through lending/investment relationships
sectorScopes: Real economy borrowers/investees across all sectors
archetypes.logic: Because FIs are evaluated partly on what share of their clients hold SBTi-validated targets, FIs have a direct commercial incentive to push their corporate borrowers and investees (their "supply chain" of capital recipients) to adopt science-based targets themselves
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Cascading SBT adoption through lending/investment relationships” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: % of long-term corporate loan/equity/fixed-income portfolio with SBT-holding counterparties
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Cascading SBT adoption through lending/investment relationships: % of long-term corporate loan/equity/fixed-income portfolio with SBT-holding counterparties

archetypes.name: Value-chain definition and disclosure for fossil fuel exposure
archetypes.logic: FIs must define and disclose exactly which value-chain segments (exploration, extraction, mining, power generation, refining, etc.) count toward "fossil fuel" exposure using standard industry classification codes (NAICS/GICS/SIC/NACE), creating a consistent, auditable supply-chain boundary across the finance sector
archetypes.preciseQuestion: What current, direct evidence confirms “Value-chain definition and disclosure for fossil fuel exposure” for the selected country and sector?
archetypes.evidenceTarget: Disclosed industry-code definitions for coal/oil & gas value chain; segment-level financial and GHG metrics
archetypes.exampleGuidance: Value-chain definition and disclosure for fossil fuel exposure: Disclosed industry-code definitions for coal/oil & gas value chain; segment-level financial and GHG metrics
